worth_it_explanation: Naming hygiene decides whether a publisher can slice TripleLift reporting by placement, format, and page type or whether every line collapses into one undifferentiated bucket. Tag and deal names that follow a parseable convention let the publisher read performance by the dimensions that matter and spot the inventory worth pricing higher. The check is a review of names the publisher already assigned, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a rename that pays off every time someone pulls a report.
intro: Naming conventions decide how readable a publisher's TripleLift setup is once it grows past a handful of tags and deals. A name is the only label that follows an ad tag, placement, or deal through every report, so a consistent and parseable convention lets the publisher slice revenue by placement, format, and page type, while an ad-hoc one forces guesswork. This subcategory is about the naming itself, the structure of the names and how consistently it is applied, rather than the inventory or deal mechanics those names point to. These checks confirm that tag and placement names are parseable and that deal and package names are consistent enough to identify at a glance.

audit_checks.title: Ad tag and placement names do not follow a parseable convention
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Tag and placement names that lack a consistent structure cannot be parsed in reporting, so a publisher cannot break revenue out by placement, format, or page type without manual sorting. The inventory still serves and earns, but the publisher loses the ability to read which slices perform and which need attention, which slows every optimization decision that depends on segmented data.

educate.base:
A name is the label that follows an ad tag or placement through every report a publisher pulls. When that name carries structure, reporting can read the structure back out. A name like 'homepage_top_native_desktop' tells anyone reading a report the page, the position, the format, and the device without a lookup, and reporting tools can group by any one of those parts. A name like 'tag3' or 'new_placement_final' tells the reader nothing, so the data behind it can only be read as one undifferentiated line.

TripleLift is compatible with Prebid, and the same naming discipline that Prebid documents for header-bidding ad units applies to the tags and placements a publisher sets up here. Prebid's ad unit 'code' is the identifier that links an ad slot to the ad server, and Prebid's guidance notes that publishers who reuse the same name across slots, or assign names with no descriptive content, make that identifier hard to use for reporting and targeting. The Prebid Ad Slot and the GPID conventions exist precisely so a slot carries a descriptive and stable label rather than an opaque one.

A parseable convention is a name built from ordered parts joined by a consistent separator, so that each part means the same thing in the same position every time. The exact parts depend on the inventory, but a publisher usually wants the page or section, the placement position, the format, e.g., native, display, or video, and the device. The value is not the structure for its own sake. It is that the structure lets the publisher answer questions like which placements earn the most, which formats clear higher, and which page types carry the strong inventory, all from reporting rather than from memory.

A few signs point to names that do not follow a parseable convention:
  • Tag and placement names mix structured and ad-hoc forms, e.g., 'article_mid_native' next to 'test2' and 'JohnsTag'
  • The same descriptive part appears in different positions or with different separators across names, so a report cannot group on it
  • Names carry no descriptive content, so reading a report requires a separate lookup to learn what each tag is
You may want to confirm that tag and placement names follow one documented convention applied across the inventory. One approach is to agree on the ordered parts and the separator, write them down, and rename existing tags to match so reporting can slice on every part.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the inventory spans many sections, page templates, and positions, so the value of a parseable name compounds. You may want the convention to capture the section and the template, e.g., 'sports_article_inline_native', so a report can compare how the same format performs across sections and surface the inventory worth pricing higher.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, editorial pages and commercial pages reach audiences at different distances from a purchase, so the page type belongs in the name. You may want the convention to mark whether a tag sits on a product, category, or editorial page, so reporting can separate the higher-intent commercial inventory from the editorial inventory rather than blending them.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Ad Slot and GPID for Header Bidding
Ad Unit Reference for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review the names of the ad tags and placements in the TripleLift setup, and check whether they share one parseable structure or mix structured and ad-hoc forms.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift publisher console
steps: Open the inventory or placement section of the TripleLift publisher console and list the active ad tags and placements with their names.
Read the names as a set and note whether they share a consistent structure of ordered parts and one separator, or whether the forms vary.
Pick a dimension the publisher would want to report on, e.g., format or page type, and check whether every name carries that part in the same position so a report could group on it.
Record any names with no descriptive content or with parts in inconsistent positions since those are the ones that break parseable reporting.

spreadsheet_review.label: Spreadsheet review
steps: Export or copy the tag and placement names into a spreadsheet, one name per row.
Split each name on its separator into columns and check whether the same meaning lands in the same column across rows.
Flag rows where a column holds the wrong kind of value or is empty, which marks the names that do not follow the convention.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the ad tag and placement names follow one parseable convention, with ordered parts in consistent positions and a single separator across the inventory. A set of names that a report can group on by every intended dimension passes. A set that mixes structured and ad-hoc forms, places the same part inconsistently, or carries names with no descriptive content is a fail recorded with the names that break the convention.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the tag and placement names follow one parseable convention above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} names its TripleLift ad tags and placements without a consistent parseable convention, so reporting cannot slice revenue by placement, format, or page type without manual sorting. A name is the label that follows a tag through every report, and names that carry no structure can only be read as one undifferentiated line. {{voice_recommend}} agreeing on an ordered set of parts, e.g., page, position, format, and device, with one separator, documenting it, and renaming the existing tags to match. After the rename, confirm that a report can group on each part so the convention earns its keep every time someone pulls the numbers.

audit_checks.title: Deal and package names are not consistent or identifiable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Deal and package names that vary in form or carry no identifying content force a publisher to look up which buyer, format, or inventory each deal covers before they can read its performance. The deals still transact, but inconsistent names slow troubleshooting and reconciliation, and they raise the chance of crediting a result to the wrong deal when names look alike.

educate.base:
A deal name is how a publisher recognizes a programmatic deal across the systems that touch it. The same deal appears in TripleLift reporting, in the publisher's own records, and in the buyer's platform on the other side, and the name is the human-readable handle that ties those views together. When deal and package names are consistent and carry identifying content, the publisher can read a report and know which buyer, which inventory, and which format a deal covers without a lookup. When names vary in form or carry nothing identifying, every read of the data starts with a hunt.

The cost shows up most in troubleshooting and reconciliation. If a deal underdelivers or a number does not match the buyer's count, the publisher has to find the deal first, and names like 'Deal 4' or 'Q3test' make that slow. Names that look alike across deals raise a further risk, which is crediting a result to the wrong deal because two entries were hard to tell apart. A consistent convention, e.g., buyer, inventory, and format in a fixed order, removes both problems.

The wider industry has been moving toward standard deal identification for the same reason. IAB Tech Lab's Deals API was created to give buyers and sellers a common way to communicate deal information, after years in which mismatched deal formats caused failed auctions and deals that were hard to diagnose. A publisher cannot impose an industry standard on its own, but the same principle applies inside one account, which is that a deal is only as easy to manage as its name is to identify.

A few signs point to deal and package names that are not consistent or identifiable:
  • Deal names mix forms across the account, e.g., a descriptive 'AcmeMedia_homepage_native' next to a bare 'Deal 12'
  • Names carry no buyer, inventory, or format content, so reading a report requires a lookup to learn what each deal is
  • Two or more deals carry names so similar that a reader could credit a result to the wrong one
You may want to confirm that deal and package names follow one convention that identifies the buyer, the inventory, and the format. One approach is to document the convention, rename the existing deals to match, and use the same form for every new deal going forward.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, deals often target specific sections or content categories, so the section belongs in the deal name alongside the buyer and format. You may want a convention like 'buyer_section_format' so a report makes clear which deal runs against which part of the site and a section-level underdelivery is easy to trace.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals against commercial pages carry different value than deals against editorial pages, so the page type belongs in the name. You may want the convention to mark whether a deal targets commercial or editorial inventory, so the publisher can tell at a glance which deals run against the higher-intent pages.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed configuration documentation for deals and packages is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the names of the deals and packages in the TripleLift setup, and check whether they follow one convention that identifies the buyer, inventory, and format.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift publisher console
steps: Open the deals or packages section of the TripleLift publisher console and list the active deals with their names and identifiers.
Read the names as a set and note whether they share a consistent structure that identifies the buyer, the inventory, and the format.
Flag any name that carries no identifying content, e.g., 'Deal 12' or a bare date. Such a name forces a lookup before a report can be read.
Check for pairs of names that are similar enough that a reader could confuse them, and record them as a risk for misattributed results.

spreadsheet_review.label: Spreadsheet review
steps: Copy the deal and package names and their identifiers into a spreadsheet, one deal per row.
Note for each whether the name states the buyer, the inventory, and the format, and whether the form matches the others.
Mark the deals whose names are bare, inconsistent, or easily confused with another, which are the ones the convention should fix.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the deal and package names follow one convention that identifies the buyer, inventory, and format in a consistent form across the account. A set of names a reader can identify at a glance, with no confusable pairs, passes. A set that mixes forms, carries names with no identifying content, or includes names so similar they risk misattribution is a fail recorded with the deals that break the convention.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the deal and package names follow one identifiable convention above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} names its TripleLift deals and packages without a consistent, identifiable convention, so reading or reconciling a deal starts with a lookup rather than the name itself. A deal name is the handle that ties the publisher's view, TripleLift reporting, and the buyer's records together, and bare or inconsistent names slow troubleshooting and raise the chance of crediting a result to the wrong deal. {{voice_recommend}} documenting a convention that states the buyer, the inventory, and the format in a fixed order, renaming the existing deals to match, and applying the same form to every new deal. After the rename, confirm that no two deal names are easy to confuse so reconciliation stays clean.
